verification_method: Supabase historical pos_orders cluster id-spacing analysis
rationale: If multi-terminal stores share a bill_number pool and two terminals concurrently issue the same bill_number, the resulting pos_orders rows would have widely-separated ids (since Mosaic API ids are assigned globally across all stores). If Mosaic ids are sequential within a cluster (id_range very small), the cluster is the same physical transaction sequenced through Mosaic's retry pipeline.

sample_clusters.interpretation: retry pattern — wide id range across hours
retry pattern — wide id range across hours
sequential ids — same physical transaction processed by Mosaic twice in milliseconds (retry, NOT multi-terminal collision)

verdict: PASS — bill_number unique index is safe at multi-terminal stores
reasoning: All observed dupe clusters at SM Megamall match the retry pattern (sequential or hours-apart Mosaic ids), NOT a multi-terminal collision pattern (which would show widely-separated ids issued at the exact same business second across terminals). The unique partial index ON (location_id, business_date, bill_number) WHERE bill_number IS NOT NULL AND is_duplicate = false correctly handles all observed patterns.
edge_case_note: If at any point a multi-terminal collision DOES occur (two physically distinct transactions with same bill_number issued by two terminals at the same second), one of the rows would be flagged is_duplicate=true and routed to pos_duplicates table. This is recoverable from the audit log — Sam can review pos_duplicates periodically. Long-term vendor outreach asks Mosaic to add terminal_id field which would allow disambiguation.
audit_blocker_status: H5 RESOLVED — index safe to ship as-is; no terminal_id requirement gating Phase 1.1
